chapter.title: Using MessageTrans
chapter.summary: English isn’t the first language of all RISC OS users, so how can we cater for those who would prefer to use our application in a different one?

section.p: Despite considering quite a few aspects of our application, one thing that we haven’t yet thought about is the possibility that anyone might wish to use it in a language other than English. And whilst English appears to be the default language for many RISC OS applications, a significant number of users will be using a different one day to day. It would clearly be desirable to allow our application to translated for use in different languages, so that even if we can’t do it ourselves, other people can provide translations if they wish to do so.

Unfortunately, there’s a problem with this: we can translate the window and icon text in the Templates, and the contents of the !Help file, but there is are several pieces of user-facing text embedded into the !RunImage file itself. Translating this would seem to require us to build a new copy of the application for each target language, or at least engage in some complicated conditional logic for selecting the appropriate pieces of text.

section.title: The Messages file
section.p: Fortunately RISC OS has a better solution, in the form of the MessageTrans module. This provides a number of SWI calls which allow us to move all of our user-facing text out of the !RunImage file and into a separate text file, then look the text up using simple tokens. We can then add this file to the list for translation, alongside Templates and !Help, should another language be requested.

The first thing that we will need to do is to create our Messages file. Looking through the source files for text that will be presented to the user, we find the application and sprite names in c.main as seen in .

The application name is allocated, so this can’t change in translastion, and the sprite name isn’t user-facing. Further down the file, shows an error message used when the sprites are being loaded. Any error here would probably occur whilst the application was initialising itself and its messages, so we will leave this untouched as well: an error in English is better than no error at all if MessageTrans fails to work as expected!

Moving on to c.ibar, we start by loading the template for the program information window and reporting an error if this fails, as seen in . By this stage, MessageTrans should safely be running, so there’s no harm in making this message available for translastion.
Similarly, we can also include the message used if the iconbar menu fails to build. We also have the menu title passed to menu_create() and the menu entries passed to menu_entry(), as seen in .
Moving on to c.win, we again have an error if the main window template fails to load, as seen in .
And once again we have errors and menu components for the window menu and the shapes pop-up menu as seen in .
Finally, in c.calc, shows the “n/a” which is used to denote for “not applicable”.

Pulling all of these pieces of text together, we end up with the Messages file seen in . The format is fairly simple: lines consist of tokens – which can be any character except a comma (,), closing bracket ()), forward slash (/) or question mark (?) until the first colon (:) is reached. Following the colon is the value associated with the token. Blank lines, and lines starting with a #, are ignored.
For example, the token “MainMenu2” in the file below would be associated with the text “3 Decimal Places”, while the token “NA” would be associated with the text “n/a”. The tokens are case sensitive.

code.title: The new Messages file for MessageTrans

section.code:
# Example 29.1 # # (c) Stephen Fryatt, 2026 # # File: Messages
# Calculation messages
NA:n/a
# Iconbar menu
IBarMenuTitle:Example IBarMenu0:Info IBarMenu1:Help... IBarMenu2:Quit
# Main menu
MainMenuTitle:Example MainMenu0:1 Decimal place MainMenu1:2 Decimal places MainMenu2:3 Decimal places
# Shapes menu
ShapeMenuTitle:Shapes ShapeMenu0:Circle ShapeMenu1:Triangle ShapeMenu2:Square
# Error messages
BadIBarMenu:Failed to create Iconbar Menu BadMainMenu:Failed to create Main Menu BadShapeMenu:Failed to create Shapes Menu
BadMainTempl:Failed to load Main template BadInfoTempl:Failed to load ProgInfo template
section.p: There are a couple of additional wrinkles to the file format to be borne in mind, although we aren’t using them here. The ? character is treated as a single-character wildcard in a token name, so the line
section.code: Help.IBar.Menu?:This is an entry in the Icon Bar menu.
section.p: would match “Help.IBarMenu0”, “Help.IBarMenu1”, “Help.IBarMenuA”, and a whole lot more variations. It is also possible to specify complete alternative tokens, using either / or a newline as separators – both
section.code: Help.Find.Cancel Help.Goto.Cancel Help.Sort.Cancel:Click Select to close the dialogue.
section.p: would be equivalent. In both cases, three separate tokens would return the text “Click Select to close the dialogue.”; this can be extremely useful when working with things like repetitive interactive help messages.
